limitations: The abstract does not report quantitative evaluations or benchmarks, so the performance gains and trade-offs of Telco-RAG versus baselines are unclear.
The work appears focused primarily on 3GPP and telecom standards, so additional adaptation may be needed before applying Telco-RAG to non-standards or less formally-structured technical corpora.
notes.relevance_reason: This paper presents a domain-specific RAG framework and practical guidelines for implementing RAG on complex technical documents, directly contributing to understanding recent advances and best practices in RAG for LLMs.

notes.title: VisRAG: Vision-based Retrieval-augmented Generation on Multi-modality Documents
notes.core_contribution: Proposes VisRAG, a vision-language-model-based retrieval-augmented generation pipeline that directly embeds multi-modal documents as images to better exploit visual and layout information for RAG.
notes.methodology: The authors build a RAG pipeline centered on a vision-language model: instead of parsing documents to text, documents are treated as images and embedded directly by a VLM to create retrieval representations; a trained retriever (using both open-source and synthetic multi-modal document data) selects relevant document images, and a VLM-based generator uses the retrieved visual context to answer queries or generate outputs, with comparisons to traditional text-based RAG in both retrieval and generation stages.
key_findings: VisRAG, which embeds entire documents as images using a VLM, outperforms traditional text-based RAG in both retrieval and generation on multi-modality document tasks.
The approach yields 20–40% end-to-end performance gains over standard text-only RAG pipelines, indicating substantial benefits from preserving visual and layout cues.
Training experiments show that VisRAG is data-efficient, achieving strong performance with relatively limited training data.
VisRAG demonstrates good generalization capability across different multi-modal document scenarios when trained on a mix of open-source and synthetic data.
limitations: The system and experiments focus specifically on multi-modality documents and may not directly transfer to purely textual corpora.
Reliance on VLMs and image-based embeddings may increase computational cost compared to lightweight text-only RAG pipelines.
Details on robustness to noisy scans/OCR quality, extremely long documents, or domain shift beyond the tested datasets are not fully clear from the abstract.
notes.relevance_reason: Highly relevant because it presents a recent RAG architecture tailored to multi-modal documents, extending standard text-based RAG with vision-language modeling and reporting concrete performance gains.

notes.title: Long$^2$RAG: Evaluating Long-Context & Long-Form Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Key Point Recall
notes.core_contribution: Introduces Long$^2$RAG, a benchmark and the Key Point Recall (KPR) metric for evaluating long-context retrieval and long-form generation capabilities in RAG systems.
notes.methodology: The authors construct Long$^2$RAG, a benchmark of 280 questions over 10 domains and 8 question types, each paired with five long retrieved documents (~2,444 words each) to emulate realistic RAG retrieval contexts, and design the Key Point Recall (KPR) metric that first extracts key points from retrieved documents and then measures the proportion of these points that are incorporated into LLM-generated long-form answers, thereby evaluating both long-context retrieval handling and exploitation of retrieved content.
key_findings: Current RAG benchmarks inadequately assess LLMs’ ability to handle long-context retrieval due to missing realistic long retrieved documents.
Existing evaluations fail to capture how well models leverage retrieved information in long-form generation, motivating the KPR metric based on incorporation of key points from documents.

Long$^2$RAG provides 280 questions across 10 domains and 8 question categories, each with 5 long retrieved documents (average 2,444 words), enabling realistic stress-testing of RAG systems’ long-context and long-form abilities.
The proposed KPR metric offers a more nuanced assessment of how effectively generated responses exploit key information from retrieved documents compared to prior coarse-grained metrics.
limitations: The benchmark size (280 questions) is relatively small, which may limit coverage of all possible RAG use cases.
The abstract does not discuss cross-lingual or multimodal scenarios, suggesting the benchmark may focus only on monolingual text settings.
KPR focuses on recall of predefined key points and may not fully capture other important aspects of generation quality, such as coherence, reasoning, or factual robustness beyond the key points.
notes.relevance_reason: Highly relevant because it proposes a new benchmark and metric specifically for evaluating long-context and long-form retrieval-augmented generation with LLMs, directly advancing methods to assess and improve modern RAG systems.

notes.title: Probabilistic distances-based hallucination detection in LLMs with RAG
notes.core_contribution: Proposes a probabilistic distance-based method that detects hallucinations in LLMs within RAG setups by measuring distributional distances between prompt token embeddings and response token embeddings.
notes.methodology: The authors model hallucination detection as a probabilistic comparison between the distributions of token embeddings from the input prompt (including retrieved evidence in a RAG setup) and those from the LLM’s generated response, computing distributional distance measures over hidden state geometries to infer a factuality signal; they evaluate this unsupervised detector (leveraging transfer from NLI) on hallucination detection benchmarks and RAG scenarios, emphasizing scalability to long sequences.
key_findings: Hallucinations persist in LLMs even under retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) settings, underscoring the need for RAG-specific detection approaches.
A distance-based analysis of the geometric structure of prompt and response hidden states provides a reliable factuality signal suitable for long sequences.
The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance on hallucination detection benchmarks compared to existing approaches.
The approach can be transferred from natural language inference (NLI) training to hallucination detection, enabling a fully unsupervised and efficient detector with competitive performance.
limitations: The abstract does not describe performance across different LLM architectures or domains, so generality may be limited.
Details on failure cases or sensitivity to different types of hallucinations (e.g., subtle vs. blatant factual errors) are not provided in the abstract.
The method appears focused on detection rather than mitigation or correction of hallucinations.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper directly targets hallucination detection in LLMs specifically within RAG settings, providing a new probabilistic embedding-distance method that advances reliability for RAG-based generation.

notes.title: Federated Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Systematic Mapping Study
notes.core_contribution: This paper provides the first systematic mapping study of Federated Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Federated RAG), organizing and analyzing existing work from 2020–2025 in terms of research focus, contributions, architectures, and challenges.
notes.methodology: The authors follow Kitchenham’s guidelines for evidence-based software engineering to conduct a systematic mapping study of Federated RAG research from 2020 to 2025, including structured literature search, selection, and classification of studies by research focus, contribution type, application domain, architectural patterns, and reported challenges.
key_findings: Federated RAG is emerging as a key framework for applying retrieval-augmented language models in privacy-sensitive domains such as healthcare, finance, and personalized assistants.

Existing work clusters around recurring architectural patterns that combine federated learning with retrieval mechanisms, revealing common design choices for privacy-preserving, knowledge‑intensive NLP.

Major technical challenges include privacy-preserving retrieval, managing cross-client data and system heterogeneity, and the lack of robust, standardized evaluation methodologies for Federated RAG systems.
Temporal trend analysis shows a rapidly growing research interest in Federated RAG, but with many open questions and incomplete solutions at the intersection of RAG and federated systems.
limitations: As a mapping study, the paper synthesizes existing work but does not propose or empirically evaluate a new Federated RAG model or algorithm.
The analysis is limited to literature published between 2020 and 2025, so more recent developments are not covered.
Findings depend on the completeness and quality of the surveyed studies, and evaluation-related conclusions may be constrained by the limited and heterogeneous benchmarks used in prior work.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper directly surveys and structures the state of the art in Federated Retrieval-Augmented Generation, a specialized but important subset of recent RAG advances for large language models, highlighting architectures and challenges relevant to the broader RAG research question.

notes.title: Is Conformal Factuality for RAG-based LLMs Robust? Novel Metrics and Systematic Insights
notes.core_contribution: This paper systematically evaluates conformal factuality filtering in RAG-based LLMs, introducing informativeness-aware metrics and robustness analyses, and provides practical guidance for building reliable and efficient RAG pipelines.
notes.methodology: The authors conduct a systematic empirical study of conformal factuality filtering in RAG-based LLMs, defining new informativeness-aware evaluation metrics, and evaluating different scoring strategies (LLM-based confidence vs. lightweight entailment-based verifiers) across three benchmarks and multiple LLM families under various conditions, including distribution shifts and distractor-laden retrieval, to assess factuality, informativeness, robustness, and computational efficiency.
key_findings: Conformal factuality filtering often yields very low-utility outputs at high factuality levels because aggressive filtering leads to vacuous or overly terse responses.

The statistical guarantees of conformal factuality are fragile under distribution shifts and in the presence of irrelevant distractor evidence, as guarantees rely heavily on calibration data closely matching deployment conditions.

Lightweight entailment-based verifier models can match or surpass LLM-based confidence scorers in factuality filtering performance while using over 100× fewer FLOPs, enabling more efficient RAG systems.

There is an inherent trade-off between factuality and informativeness in current conformal filtering frameworks, motivating new approaches that jointly optimize robustness, usefulness, and computational efficiency.
limitations: The conformal factuality guarantees critically depend on the calibration data being closely aligned with deployment distributions, limiting robustness to real-world shifts.
The study is constrained to three benchmarks and a finite set of model families, so findings may not fully generalize to all RAG tasks or future larger models.
The work focuses on conformal filtering and does not propose a fully new reliability framework, instead mainly analyzing and stress-testing existing approaches.
notes.relevance_reason: Highly relevant because it analyzes and improves reliability and robustness techniques specifically for RAG-based LLMs, introducing metrics and practical insights directly applicable to recent advances in RAG.

notes.title: EVOR: Evolving Retrieval for Code Generation
notes.core_contribution: Introduces EVOR, a retrieval-augmented code generation pipeline that synchronously evolves both queries and diverse, multi-source knowledge bases to better support LLMs on domains with insufficient built-in knowledge.
notes.methodology: The authors design EVOR, a retrieval-augmented code generation pipeline where both user queries and the external knowledge base co-evolve: queries are iteratively refined to better express information needs, while the knowledge base is dynamically expanded and updated from diverse sources such as documentation and other external resources. They construct EVOR-BENCH, four datasets centered on frequently updated libraries and long-tail programming languages, and compare EVOR against baseline RACG and prompting methods (e.g., Reflexion, DocPrompting) by measuring execution accuracy on code generation tasks that explicitly require external knowledge. They further analyze ablations to quantify the contributions of synchronous query/document evolution and knowledge-source diversity.
key_findings: EVOR, which jointly evolves queries and documents, substantially improves execution accuracy (2–4x) over existing retrieval-augmented code generation and prompting methods such as Reflexion and DocPrompting on tasks requiring external knowledge.

Constructing EVOR-BENCH with frequently updated libraries and long-tail programming languages shows that standard RACG pipelines with static, single-source knowledge bases struggle to adapt to dynamic or niche domains.

EVOR’s use of diverse information sources in the knowledge base (rather than a single, static source) is a key factor in its performance gains on realistic code generation settings requiring external knowledge.
EVOR can be flexibly combined with other methods (e.g., Reflexion, DocPrompting) to obtain further improvements beyond either approach alone.
limitations: The approach and evaluations are focused on code generation tasks and may not directly generalize to non-code RAG scenarios without adaptation.
Performance is demonstrated primarily on the newly introduced EVOR-BENCH datasets, so the extent of generalization to other benchmarks and industrial settings requires further validation.
Maintaining and evolving diverse knowledge bases (frequently updated libraries, long-tail language resources) may introduce additional system complexity and engineering overhead.
notes.relevance_reason: Highly relevant as it presents a recent, domain-specific advance in retrieval-augmented generation for LLMs, showing how dynamic, multi-source knowledge bases and co-evolving queries can significantly improve performance in code generation tasks.

notes.title: Is My Data in Your Retrieval Database? Membership Inference Attacks Against Retrieval Augmented Generation
notes.core_contribution: Introduces and empirically evaluates a practical membership inference attack method against retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, along with an initial prompt-based defense strategy.
notes.methodology: Designs a membership inference attack that crafts targeted prompts to probe whether specific documents are contained in a RAG system’s retrieval database, evaluates its success across two benchmark datasets and multiple generative LLM backends under black-box and gray-box access assumptions, and tests a defense that augments the RAG prompt/template with explicit privacy-preserving instructions to reduce leakages.
key_findings: A simple, efficient prompting-based membership inference attack can determine whether a document is present in a RAG retrieval database by only observing the system’s outputs.
The proposed attack is effective across two benchmark datasets and multiple underlying generative models, and works in both black-box and gray-box threat settings.
RAG systems leak information about the membership of documents in their retrieval databases, posing significant privacy and security risks when the database includes proprietary or sensitive content.

Adding privacy-oriented instructions to the RAG template can substantially mitigate membership inference in some datasets and models, but is not a universal or fully robust defense, underscoring the need for stronger protections.
limitations: Defense strategy is limited to instruction-level modifications of the RAG template and may not generalize across all datasets, models, or stronger attackers.
Evaluation, as described in the abstract, is conducted on two benchmark datasets and a finite set of generative models, so results may not cover all RAG configurations used in practice.
The work focuses on membership inference attacks, not on broader classes of privacy or security vulnerabilities in RAG (e.g., data reconstruction or attribute inference).
notes.relevance_reason: Highly relevant as it studies privacy vulnerabilities and defenses specific to retrieval-augmented generation systems, which are a key aspect of recent advances and practical deployment considerations for RAG with large language models.

notes.title: DCMI: A Differential Calibration Membership Inference Attack Against Retrieval-Augmented Generation
notes.core_contribution: Proposes DCMI, a differential calibration-based membership inference attack specifically tailored to RAG systems to more accurately infer whether documents in the external knowledge base were used in training or are members of the database.
notes.methodology: The authors design a differential calibration membership inference attack (DCMI) that constructs perturbed versions of user queries to probe a RAG system, then measures the sensitivity gap in system responses when member vs. non-member documents are retrieved; by calibrating on these perturbations, the attack isolates the influence of member documents while suppressing noise from non-member-retrieved documents, and is empirically evaluated against baselines on both controlled RAG setups (e.g., Flan-T5) and real-world RAG platforms (Dify, MaxKB).
key_findings: DCMI leverages the sensitivity gap between member and non-member retrieved documents under query perturbations, enabling more precise membership inference in RAG systems.
By generating perturbed queries for calibration, DCMI isolates the contribution of member-retrieved documents and reduces interference from non-member documents in the retrieval set.
Under progressively relaxed assumptions, DCMI achieves up to 97.42% AUC and 94.35% accuracy against a Flan-T5-based RAG system, outperforming the MBA baseline by over 40%.
On real-world RAG platforms such as Dify and MaxKB, DCMI maintains a 10%-20% performance advantage over baseline attacks, underscoring substantial privacy risks in deployed RAG systems.
limitations: The attack is evaluated primarily on certain model/back-end configurations (e.g., Flan-T5-based RAG), so its effectiveness on other architectures or in more diverse, large-scale production settings may vary.
The approach assumes the ability to issue multiple perturbed queries to the RAG system, which might be restricted or monitored in some real-world deployments.
The abstract highlights privacy risks but does not propose concrete defense mechanisms, leaving mitigation strategies for future work.
notes.relevance_reason: Highly relevant as it studies privacy and membership inference vulnerabilities specific to retrieval-augmented generation systems, a critical recent dimension of RAG research, though it focuses on attacks rather than general architectural or performance advances.

notes.title: RAG Security and Privacy: Formalizing the Threat Model and Attack Surface
notes.core_contribution: Proposes the first formal threat model and structured taxonomy for security and privacy risks specific to retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, including formal definitions of key attack vectors.
notes.methodology: Conceptual and formal analysis: the authors construct a formal threat model for retrieval-based RAG systems, develop a taxonomy of adversaries based on access levels to components and data, and provide formal definitions and attack models for key threats such as document-level membership inference and data poisoning in RAG pipelines.
key_findings: Identifies that RAG systems inherit known LLM vulnerabilities such as training data memorization and adversarial prompting while also introducing new risks due to their reliance on external knowledge bases.
Defines a structured taxonomy of adversaries for RAG based on their access to model components and data, enabling systematic analysis of different attack scenarios.
Formally characterizes document-level membership inference in RAG as a primary privacy risk, where attackers can infer the presence or absence of specific documents in the retrieval corpus.

Formally defines and analyzes data poisoning threats in RAG, highlighting how adversaries can inject malicious or corrupted content into the knowledge base to manipulate outputs and compromise integrity.
limitations: The abstract does not describe empirical evaluations or real-world case studies validating the proposed threat model.
The work appears to focus on defining threats rather than proposing or evaluating concrete defense mechanisms.
The scope seems limited to retrieval-based RAG architectures and may not cover other hybrid LLM-augmentation paradigms.
notes.relevance_reason: Highly relevant because it systematically analyzes and formalizes the security and privacy threat landscape for RAG systems, which is a critical dimension of recent advances in RAG for large language models.

notes.title: Mechanistic Understanding and Mitigation of Language Model Non-Factual Hallucinations
notes.core_contribution: This paper provides a mechanistic analysis of non-factual hallucinations in large language models and introduces a targeted internal intervention method to mitigate these hallucinations by restoring the model’s fact recall pipeline.
notes.methodology: The authors construct diagnostic datasets consisting of subject–relation queries and apply adapted mechanistic interpretability techniques to trace how factual information is processed through different layers and components (MLPs vs. attention heads) in several LMs (Llama-2, Pythia, GPT-J), identifying where information is lost or distorted. Based on this analysis, they design a targeted intervention that restores or corrects internal representations along the fact recall pipeline to reduce hallucinations, and empirically compare this method against baseline hallucination mitigation approaches.
key_findings: Non-factual hallucinations in LMs arise from two distinct mechanistic causes shared across architectures: (1) knowledge enrichment hallucinations caused by insufficient subject attribute knowledge in lower-layer MLPs and (2) answer extraction hallucinations due to failures in upper-layer attention heads to select the correct object attribute.
These internal mechanistic causes have observable external behavioral signatures, allowing hallucination types to be linked to specific internal processing failures.
Adapting interpretability methods to trace information flow through internal representations enables diagnosis of where in the network factual information is lost or corrupted.
A targeted restoration method that intervenes on the LM’s internal fact recall pipeline reduces non-factual hallucinations more effectively than baseline mitigation techniques.
limitations: The analysis is constrained to a few model families (Llama-2, Pythia, GPT-J) and may not fully generalize to all modern or larger architectures.
The diagnostic datasets use subject–relation queries, which may not capture all forms of hallucinations seen in open-ended generation tasks.
The proposed mitigation method relies on access to internal activations and specialized interpretability tooling, which may not be feasible in closed-source or deployment-constrained settings.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper is highly relevant because it analyzes and mitigates hallucinations—one of the core challenges for retrieval-augmented generation systems—by offering mechanistic insights and intervention methods that can inform more reliable RAG pipelines, even though it does not focus specifically on retrieval augmentation.

notes.title: Enterprise AI Must Enforce Participant-Aware Access Control
notes.core_contribution: Proposes and deploys a participant-aware access control framework that enforces deterministic, fine-grained authorization over all data used in fine-tuning and RAG-based inference in multi-user enterprise LLM systems to prevent sensitive information leakage.
notes.methodology: Conceptual and system-design methodology: the authors analyze data exfiltration attacks on enterprise AI assistants using current fine-tuning and RAG pipelines, evaluate why existing defenses fail, and then propose and implement a participant-aware, deterministic access control framework that constrains which documents and training data can be used during fine-tuning and at RAG-based inference time, ensuring that all content is authorized for all parties in a given interaction; this framework is integrated into a real product (Microsoft Copilot Tuning).
key_findings: Current fine-tuning and RAG architectures in enterprise AI assistants enable data exfiltration attacks where adversaries can elicit sensitive training or retrieved content due to missing access control enforcement.

Existing defenses such as prompt sanitization, output filtering, system isolation, and training-level privacy mechanisms are inherently probabilistic and cannot guarantee robust protection against motivated attackers.

Deterministic enforcement of fine-grained access control across training, retrieval, and generation—requiring that all content involved be authorized for all participants in the interaction—provides a principled way to prevent unauthorized leakage.
The proposed participant-aware access control framework is practically viable and has been deployed in Microsoft Copilot Tuning for securely fine-tuning models on enterprise-specific data.
limitations: The abstract does not quantify security guarantees or empirical attack success reduction under the proposed framework.
Details on performance overhead, usability trade-offs, and handling complex real-world permission models are not provided in the abstract.
The position focuses on access control and may not address other vectors such as covert channels or side-information attacks beyond the described architecture.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper focuses on security and access control in enterprise LLM deployments that use RAG pipelines, providing insights into how RAG architectures can leak data and how to redesign them with participant-aware access control, which is relevant to understanding robust and secure advances in RAG for LLMs.

notes.title: Are Hallucinations Bad Estimations?
notes.core_contribution: Formalizes hallucinations in generative models as inevitable failures of optimal estimators to link outputs to plausible causes, providing theoretical lower bounds on hallucination rates and empirical validation across tasks.
notes.methodology: The paper defines hallucinations as outputs that cannot be linked to any plausible cause within the underlying data-generating distribution, then analyzes generic estimators under loss minimization to derive a high-probability lower bound on hallucination rates, and validates the theory through experiments on synthetic coin aggregation, open-ended question answering, and text-to-image generation tasks.
key_findings: Hallucinations are reframed as structural misalignments between loss-minimizing estimation and human-acceptable outputs, rather than mere optimization failures.
Even theoretically optimal, loss-minimizing estimators necessarily hallucinate under generic data distributions, with a provable high-probability lower bound on hallucination rates.
Hallucinations can be interpreted as estimation errors induced by miscalibration between model predictions and true data-generating processes.
Empirical studies on coin aggregation, open-ended QA, and text-to-image tasks support the theoretical predictions about persistent hallucination rates.
limitations: The theoretical analysis is abstract and may not capture all practical nuances of large-scale neural generative models.
Experiments are limited to coin aggregation, open-ended QA, and text-to-image settings, which may not cover the full diversity of real-world deployment scenarios.
The work characterizes inevitability and structure of hallucinations but does not provide concrete mitigation strategies for practical systems like RAG pipelines.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper studies the fundamental nature and inevitability of hallucinations in generative models, which is conceptually important for designing and understanding RAG systems that aim to reduce hallucinations, but it does not specifically analyze or propose RAG methods.

notes.title: Riddle Me This! Stealthy Membership Inference for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
notes.core_contribution: Introduces Interrogation Attack (IA), a stealthy and efficient membership inference method that uses natural-text queries to determine whether specific documents are present in a RAG datastore.
notes.methodology: The authors design Interrogation Attack (IA), which constructs natural, seemingly benign queries whose answers depend critically on whether a specific target document is retrievable by the RAG system. By adaptively crafting and issuing around 30 such queries, they observe the LLM’s responses and use statistical decision rules to infer the presence or absence of the target document in the datastore. They evaluate IA against prior membership inference and data extraction attacks across diverse RAG architectures and apply prompt-detection baselines to measure stealthiness and quantify costs on real LLM APIs.
key_findings: Interrogation Attack (IA) can successfully infer membership of target documents in a RAG datastore using only about 30 natural-language queries per document.
IA remains stealthy compared to prior methods: simple adversarial-prompt detectors flag existing membership inference and extraction attacks up to ~76× more often than IA’s queries.
IA achieves roughly 2× improvement in true positive rate at 1% false positive rate (TPR@1%FPR) over prior membership inference attacks across a variety of RAG system configurations.
The proposed attack is cost-effective, with an average cost of less than $0.02 per document inference in evaluated settings.
limitations: The approach targets membership inference on documents in the RAG datastore and may not directly apply to non-RAG or non-retrieval settings.
Effectiveness and stealthiness are evaluated under specific RAG configurations and detectors; real-world systems with stronger defenses or different retrieval architectures may reduce attack success.
The method assumes the ability to issue multiple (around 30) queries per target document, which might be constrained in some production systems due to rate limiting or monitoring.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper focuses on a security/privacy aspect of RAG—membership inference attacks on RAG datastores—rather than improving core retrieval or generation quality, making it relevant as a recent RAG development but only indirectly to advances in RAG techniques themselves.

notes.title: SMA: Who Said That? Auditing Membership Leakage in Semi-Black-box RAG Controlling
notes.core_contribution: Introduces Source-aware Membership Audit (SMA), a semi-black-box auditing framework that attributes generated RAG/MRAG outputs to specific data sources (pre-training, retrieval, or user input) for fine-grained membership leakage analysis.
notes.methodology: The paper designs a Source-aware Membership Audit (SMA) framework for semi-black-box RAG/MRAG systems with retrieval control: it estimates token-level influence on generated outputs using a zero-order optimization scheme that relies on large-scale perturbation sampling and ridge regression models, and extends this to multimodal settings by first converting image inputs into textual descriptions via multimodal LLMs so that all modalities can be analyzed in a unified text-token attribution space for source-specific membership inference.
key_findings: Demonstrates that existing membership inference methods are insufficient for RAG/MRAG because they cannot distinguish whether content originates from pre-training data, external retrieval, or user input, hindering privacy accountability.

Proposes a zero-order optimization-based attribution estimation mechanism that approximates the influence of input tokens on outputs via large-scale perturbation sampling and ridge regression, enabling source-aware membership auditing in semi-black-box settings.

Introduces a cross-modal attribution technique that maps image inputs into textual descriptions through multimodal LLMs, enabling token-level attribution for image-based retrieval traces in MRAG systems.

Conceptually shifts membership inference from merely detecting memorization to identifying content provenance, opening a new direction for privacy and data provenance auditing in complex generative systems.
limitations: Details on empirical performance, robustness across diverse architectures, and scalability to very large retrieval corpora are not provided in the abstract.
Real-world deployment constraints, such as computational cost of large-scale perturbation sampling and potential vulnerabilities to adversarial obfuscation, are not discussed in the abstract.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper focuses on auditing membership leakage and source attribution in RAG/MRAG systems rather than on improving RAG models themselves, making it peripherally relevant as a privacy and provenance aspect of modern RAG advances.

notes.title: One SPACE to Rule Them All: Jointly Mitigating Factuality and Faithfulness Hallucinations in LLMs
notes.core_contribution: Proposes SPACE, a unified activation-space editing framework that jointly mitigates factuality and faithfulness hallucinations in LLMs by exploiting their shared neural subspaces.
notes.methodology: The paper first conducts empirical and theoretical analyses of activation space dynamics in LLMs to show that factuality and faithfulness hallucinations share overlapping neural subspaces. Building on this, it formulates a dual-task feature modeling framework that provides a geometric characterization of these shared subspaces. SPACE then uses a hybrid probing pipeline: spectral clustering over activation features to discover candidate subspaces and attention head saliency scoring to localize influential components. Finally, it edits the identified activation subspaces during inference to jointly improve factuality and faithfulness, and evaluates performance across multiple hallucination benchmarks.
key_findings: Empirical and theoretical analysis shows that factuality and faithfulness hallucinations occupy overlapping subspaces in LLM activation space, enabling joint mitigation.
SPACE introduces a dual-task feature modeling approach that provides a geometric foundation for identifying shared subspaces related to both hallucination types.
A hybrid probing strategy combining spectral clustering over activations with attention head saliency scoring can effectively locate and edit hallucination-related subspaces.

Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that SPACE outperforms existing methods on both factuality and faithfulness metrics without incurring the usual performance trade-offs between them.
limitations: The method depends on access to and manipulation of internal activations, which may be impractical for closed-source or highly constrained LLM deployments.
Details on computational overhead and scalability to very large models and task distributions are not fully clear from the abstract.
SPACE is evaluated only on benchmark datasets; its robustness and generality in diverse real-world applications remain to be validated.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper directly tackles hallucination mitigation in LLMs via activation-space editing rather than retrieval-augmented generation, making it related to factuality but not focused on advances in RAG methods.

notes.title: Membership Inference Attacks on Large-Scale Models: A Survey
notes.core_contribution: This survey provides the first comprehensive review of membership inference attacks (MIAs) on large-scale models, including LLMs, LMMs, and their full pipelines (pre-training, fine-tuning, alignment, and RAG), and outlines open challenges for improving privacy resilience.
notes.methodology: The authors conduct a structured literature survey of membership inference attacks targeting large-scale models (LLMs and LMMs), organizing prior work by model type, adversary knowledge, attack strategy, and model lifecycle stage (pre-training, fine-tuning, alignment, and RAG), and then synthesize open challenges and future research directions.
key_findings: MIAs pose significant privacy risks to large-scale models such as LLMs and LMMs, extending concerns well beyond traditional machine learning settings.
Existing MIA techniques can be systematically categorized by model type, adversary knowledge, and attack strategy when applied to large-scale models.

MIAs can target multiple stages of the large-scale model pipeline, including pre-training, fine-tuning, alignment, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), each with distinct vulnerabilities and threat models.

There remain substantial open challenges and research opportunities in designing privacy-preserving large-scale models and defenses specifically tailored to mitigate MIAs in LLMs, LMMs, and RAG systems.
limitations: The survey focuses on membership inference attacks and does not deeply analyze other types of privacy or security attacks on large-scale models.
The abstract suggests a conceptual and review-based analysis without empirical benchmarking or unified experimental comparisons across attacks.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper is only indirectly relevant to advances in RAG, focusing instead on membership inference attacks and privacy risks, though it does specifically discuss MIAs in the context of RAG pipelines as part of its broader survey.

notes.title: VLP: A Survey on Vision-Language Pre-training
notes.core_contribution: This paper provides a comprehensive survey of recent advances in vision-language pre-training (VLP) models for image-text and video-text tasks, organizing the field along key technical dimensions and outlining future research directions.
notes.methodology: The authors conduct a literature survey of recent works on vision-language pre-training, organizing and analyzing them along five perspectives—feature extraction, model architecture, pre-training objectives, datasets, and downstream tasks—then provide detailed descriptions of representative VLP models and discuss emerging research directions and challenges in the field.
key_findings: Vision-language pre-training methods can be systematically categorized and compared along five major dimensions: feature extraction strategies, model architectures, pre-training objectives, pre-training datasets, and downstream tasks.

Unified VLP frameworks have significantly advanced performance on a broad range of multimodal tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering, visual reasoning, and video understanding, demonstrating strong cross-modal transfer.

Different design choices in pre-training objectives (e.g., contrastive learning, masked modeling, cross-modal alignment) and architectures (e.g., single-stream vs. dual-stream transformers) lead to distinct trade-offs in efficiency, flexibility, and downstream task performance.

The field is rapidly evolving toward larger-scale datasets, more general-purpose multimodal models, and exploration of new frontiers such as open-world understanding, cross-lingual multimodality, and more efficient pre-training paradigms.
limitations: As a survey paper, it does not propose or empirically evaluate a new model or algorithm but synthesizes existing work.

The focus is limited to vision-language pre-training (image-text and video-text) and does not systematically cover retrieval-augmented generation frameworks or broader multimodal retrieval-generation pipelines.
Given the fast-moving nature of the field, more recent VLP and multimodal large language models developed after 2022 are not covered.
notes.relevance_reason: The paper surveys vision-language pre-training models for multimodal understanding and generation, which is adjacent to but not specifically focused on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for large language models, making it only tangentially relevant.

output.title: Recent Advances in Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models
output.abstract: This survey synthesizes recent advances in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for large language models, covering architectural foundations, empirical comparisons with long-context transformers, multimodal and code-oriented extensions, and emerging benchmarks. We analyze optimization objectives for retrievers and generators, methods for grounding and hallucination mitigation, and security, privacy, and safety considerations. The survey also reviews interactive and federated RAG paradigms, deployment and MLOps practices, and the impact of knowledge-store and chunking design on performance. Across these dimensions, we identify consistent trends toward modular, agentic, and domain-aware systems, while highlighting open challenges in robustness, evaluation, and privacy-preserving deployment.

sections.section_title: 1. Foundational RAG Models and Their Influence on Modern Architectures
sections.content:
Early retrieval-augmented generation systems established key design principles that continue to shape modern RAG for large language models. Foundational work such as REALM, RETRO, Atlas, FiD, and the original RAG variants (noted in surveys [arxiv:2405.06211v3; arxiv:2601.05264v1]) introduced the separation between a parametric generator and a non‑parametric retriever, typically based on dense passage retrieval (DPR) or similar dual-encoder architectures. These systems demonstrated that grounding generation in external corpora significantly improves factual accuracy and sample efficiency, but also exposed challenges in retrieval quality, retriever–generator mismatch, and domain adaptation.

Subsequent extensions focused on end-to-end optimization of the full pipeline. RAG-end2end [arxiv:2210.02627v1] and the HuggingFace implementation of full-architecture fine-tuning [arxiv:2106.11517v1] showed that jointly training the retriever, generator, and even the dense index leads to better alignment with domain-specific knowledge bases (e.g., COVID-19, news, conversational data). These works foreshadowed more recent LLM-aligned retrievers such as Invar-RAG [arxiv:2411.07021v2], which uses LoRA-based representation learning and an invariance loss to make LLM-based retrievers robust to input pattern variations, and domain-aware frameworks like MoLER [arxiv:2509.06650v1] that use continual pre-training and RL to specialize retrieval policies.

System-level surveys [arxiv:2601.05264v1; arxiv:2405.06211v3; arxiv:2509.10697v1] abstract these developments into a modular RAG stack: retrieval (sparse, dense, hybrid), fusion (early, late, iterative), orchestration (agents, planners, routing), and governance (trust, safety, monitoring). Blitz-style improvements in retrieval—e.g., Blended RAG’s hybrid dense+sparse search [arxiv:2404.07220v2] and enterprise-oriented hybrid pipelines in Higress-RAG [arxiv:2602.23374v1]—build directly on the foundational DPR-style retrieval while addressing recall and robustness at scale. At the same time, domain-specific systems such as T-RAG for enterprise documents [arxiv:2402.07483v2], Telco-RAG for telecom standards [arxiv:2404.15939v3], and MusT-RAG for music QA [arxiv:2507.23334v2] illustrate how early RAG principles (separate knowledge store, retrieve-then-read, joint tuning) are adapted with specialized preprocessing, indexing, and fine-tuning to meet vertical-domain needs. Overall, modern RAG architectures can be viewed as increasingly agentic, hybrid, and domain-aware elaborations of these foundational designs.

sections.section_title: 2. RAG vs. Long-Context and Hybrid Architectures: Accuracy, Cost, and Robustness
sections.content:
Recent work has systematically compared RAG pipelines with long-context transformers and hybrid systems. Stronger Baselines for RAG with Long-Context LMs [arxiv:2506.03989v2] shows that a simple retrieve-then-read baseline (DOS RAG), which preserves document order and structure, can match or outperform more complex multi-stage RAG pipelines such as ReadAgent and RAPTOR when token budgets are controlled. This suggests that some perceived gains from sophisticated orchestration actually stem from generous token budgets or better retrieval, and that high-recall, order-preserving RAG should serve as a standard baseline.

A complementary study [arxiv:2407.16833v2] benchmarks RAG against long-context (LC) LLMs across multiple datasets and models. It finds that, given large context windows and sufficient compute, LC models tend to outperform RAG in average accuracy, but at much higher computational and monetary cost. RAG, by contrast, is more cost-efficient and remains attractive when context length or budgets are constrained. The same work introduces Self-Route, a hybrid routing mechanism in which the model introspects to decide, per query, whether to invoke RAG or LC processing, achieving performance close to pure LC while significantly reducing cost.

LongRAG [arxiv:2410.18050v2] and MiA-RAG [arxiv:2512.17220v1] further blur the line between RAG and long-context reasoning by explicitly modeling both global document semantics and local evidence. LongRAG’s dual-perspective paradigm and MiA-RAG’s hierarchical "mindscape" representation show that incorporating global summaries into RAG can mitigate "lost in the middle" failures typical of LC models, while retaining the efficiency benefits of targeted retrieval. Contextual Normalization [arxiv:2510.13191v2] and Stable-RAG [arxiv:2601.02993v3] highlight that robustness in long-context settings is sensitive not only to what is retrieved, but also to formatting and permutation of retrieved chunks; simple normalization and permutation-aware aggregation significantly improve stability and factuality.

Across these studies, a consistent trade-off emerges. LC LLMs excel when budgets are unconstrained and documents are few but long; simple, strong RAG baselines dominate on cost and scalability in large-corpus settings. Hybrid routing and dual-perspective RAG designs provide a promising middle ground, but also introduce new complexity in planning and calibration. A key open challenge is developing unified evaluation protocols that jointly measure accuracy, latency, and cost under realistic deployment constraints, as advocated by systems surveys [arxiv:2601.05264v1].

sections.section_title: 3. Multimodal and Code-Oriented RAG
sections.content:
Multimodal RAG extends text-based retrieval-augmentation to vision, document layout, tables, and speech, while code-oriented RAG targets software artifacts and developer workflows. Surveys on RAS (retrieval and structuring augmented generation) [arxiv:2509.10697v1] and RA-LLMs [arxiv:2405.06211v3] emphasize structuring and heterogeneous knowledge sources as central challenges.

For visual and document-centric RAG, VisRAG [arxiv:2410.10594v2] eschews text-only pipelines by embedding entire documents as images via a vision-language model (VLM), preserving layout and visual cues that are often lost in OCR. It reports 20–40% gains over text-only RAG on multimodal document tasks. MLDocRAG [arxiv:2602.10271v3] proposes a Multimodal Chunk-Query Graph (MCQG) that links fine-grained, answerable queries to heterogeneous chunks (paragraphs, figures, tables), enabling query-centric retrieval and structured cross-modal aggregation over long documents. VisRAG and MLDocRAG both show that multimodal indexing and graph-like structures substantially improve grounding compared to naïve text conversion.

For structured data, T-RAG for tables [arxiv:2504.01346v4] introduces a hierarchical memory index and multi-stage retrieval over table corpora, plus graph-aware prompting that turns tables into graph structures for more accurate reasoning. This illustrates a general pattern: for highly structured modalities (tables, code, logs), dedicated indices and representation schemes outperform generic text chunking.

In code-oriented RAG, EVOR [arxiv:2402.12317v2] tackles domains where parametric models lack up-to-date or niche library knowledge. It synchronously evolves both user queries and the knowledge base from diverse documentation sources, demonstrating 2–4× execution accuracy gains over standard RACG and prompting-based baselines on EVOR-BENCH. EVOR’s dynamic knowledge-base evolution anticipates more general RAG pipelines for logs, traces, and continuously changing APIs. Domain-specific pipelines like MusT-RAG for music QA [arxiv:2507.23334v2] further show how specialized vector databases and joint RAG+fine-tuning can outperform pure fine-tuning or generic corpora.

Overall, modern multimodal and code/log RAG systems share two themes: (1) modality-aware indexing and representation (images, tables, code graphs, logs) and (2) structured retrieval plans or graphs that connect localized evidence nodes to user queries. This suggests that future RAG research will increasingly converge with multimodal representation learning and program analysis, rather than treating all content as flat text.

sections.section_title: 4. Benchmarks and Datasets for Evaluating RAG
sections.content:
Evaluation of RAG systems has broadened beyond traditional QA benchmarks to stress-test retrieval, multi-hop reasoning, long-context use, and multimodal grounding. The RA-LLM survey [arxiv:2405.06211v3] and the Engineering the RAG Stack review [arxiv:2601.05264v1] catalog standard QA datasets such as KILT, Natural Questions (NQ), HotpotQA, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and ASQA as core tools for measuring retrieval and answer quality.

Specialized benchmarks have emerged to probe particular failure modes. MultiHop-RAG [arxiv:2401.15391v1] focuses on multi-hop news QA, showing that both retrievers and LLMs struggle with multi-step evidence chains even when relevant passages are retrieved. MultiTableQA in T-RAG [arxiv:2504.01346v4] evaluates multi-table reasoning using a large corpus of tables and diverse tasks, highlighting the importance of table-specific indexing and graph-aware prompts. Long^2RAG [arxiv:2410.23000v3] introduces a long-context benchmark (280 questions, five long documents each) and the Key Point Recall (KPR) metric, which measures how many key points from retrieved documents are incorporated into long-form answers; this directly evaluates whether models truly exploit retrieved content.

Agentic and iterative RAG frameworks, such as RAG-Gym [arxiv:2502.13957v2] and FAIR-RAG [arxiv:2510.22344v1], provide controlled environments to benchmark retrieval policies, planning, and refinement loops. RAG-Gym standardizes the evaluation of reasoning-based agents, prompts (Re^2Search), and post-training algorithms like direct preference optimization, while FAIR-RAG assesses multi-hop QA performance under structured evidence assessment and iterative refinement.

For multimodal and document-centric RAG, MLDocRAG [arxiv:2602.10271v3] evaluates on MMLongBench-Doc and LongDocURL, while VisRAG [arxiv:2410.10594v2] uses multi-modality document datasets to demonstrate gains over text-only baselines. EVOR-BENCH [arxiv:2402.12317v2] contributes dynamic, code-oriented RAG benchmarks. Across these efforts, a trend is clear: benchmarks are becoming more realistic (long documents, heterogeneous modalities, dynamic corpora) and more diagnostic, with metrics like KPR that capture grounding quality rather than only answer correctness. However, as noted by mapping studies on federated RAG [arxiv:2505.18906v2] and RAS [arxiv:2509.10697v1], there is still a lack of standardized evaluation protocols for privacy, robustness, and cross-lingual performance.

sections.section_title: 5. Training and Optimization Objectives for Retrievers, Generators, and Policies
sections.content:
Modern RAG systems employ a rich set of training and optimization objectives that go beyond standard supervised learning. Early RAG variants used contrastive learning for retrievers (e.g., DPR) and maximum likelihood for generators, often with hard negatives sampled from the corpus, as summarized in RA-LLM and RAS surveys [arxiv:2405.06211v3; arxiv:2509.10697v1]. Joint optimization of retriever and generator, as in RAG-end2end [arxiv:2210.02627v1] and full RAG fine-tuning [arxiv:2106.11517v1], integrates retrieval supervision directly into the generative loss and updates document embeddings, improving domain adaptation at the cost of higher training complexity.

Invar-RAG [arxiv:2411.07021v2] introduces an invariance loss for LLM-based retrievers, defining variant and invariant input patterns and penalizing representational variance across them to stabilize retrieval under heterogeneous prompts. Domain-aware RAG (MoLER) [arxiv:2509.06650v1] uses a Mixture-of-Losses for continual pre-training to balance domain knowledge acquisition with general language modeling, followed by reinforcement learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization to optimize query and passage generation toward document recall. CARROT [arxiv:2411.00744v2] frames chunk selection as cost-constrained combinatorial optimization, using Monte Carlo Tree Search and a learned utility estimator to trade off token cost against non-monotonic utility of additional chunks.

Agentic RAG frameworks increasingly use preference-based and RL-style objectives over multi-step policies. RAG-Gym [arxiv:2502.13957v2] systematically compares post-training algorithms with process-level supervision, highlighting direct preference optimization (DPO) as particularly effective for tuning RAG agents. RAG-Star [arxiv:2412.12881v1] combines Monte Carlo Tree Search with retrieval-augmented verification and reward modeling to guide complex reasoning. FAIR-RAG [arxiv:2510.22344v1] implicitly optimizes an evidence sufficiency objective via iterative refinement guided by a Structured Evidence Assessment module, while Vendi-RAG [arxiv:2502.11228v2] optimizes a composite objective balancing retrieval diversity (Vendi Score) and answer quality using an LLM judge. These methods view retrieval and reasoning as a policy optimization problem rather than a single-step retrieval.

Comparatively, contrastive and hard-negative training remains efficient and well-understood, suiting static or moderately dynamic domains. Invariance-based objectives [arxiv:2411.07021v2] and mixture-of-losses continual pre-training [arxiv:2509.06650v1] improve robustness and domain specificity but require substantial data and careful regularization. RL and preference-based optimization [arxiv:2502.13957v2; arxiv:2412.12881v1; arxiv:2502.11228v2] offer strong performance on complex multi-hop tasks but introduce higher computational cost and potential instability. Designing objectives that explicitly encode cost, latency, and robustness—in addition to answer accuracy—remains an important open direction, especially for production systems highlighted in [arxiv:2601.05264v1].

sections.section_title: 6. Grounding, Attribution, and Hallucination Metrics
sections.content:
Evaluating grounding and hallucination in RAG requires metrics that go beyond simple answer correctness. Surveys on hallucination and attribution [arxiv:2510.24476v1; arxiv:2601.19927v1] propose taxonomies distinguishing knowledge-based vs. logic-based hallucinations and RAG-specific failure modes where unsupported content arises from either retrieval or generation. They emphasize citation-based metrics—citation precision/recall, support coverage, and attribution faithfulness—that measure whether generated statements are justified by retrieved evidence.

Conformal factuality filtering [arxiv:2603.16817v1] offers a principled approach by calibrating confidence scores (from entailment models or LLMs) to guarantee bounded error rates. However, empirical analysis shows a sharp factuality–informativeness trade-off: high factuality often leads to vacuous or under-informative outputs, and the guarantees are fragile under distribution shift and distractor documents. Probabilistic distance-based hallucination detection [arxiv:2506.09886v2] instead models geometric distances between prompt and response token embeddings, achieving strong unsupervised hallucination detection and scaling to long sequences, but it remains a detector rather than a full mitigation pipeline.

Permutation sensitivity and context formatting have emerged as underappreciated sources of hallucination. Stable-RAG [arxiv:2601.02993v3] documents how standard Top-5 RAG exhibits large answer variation across permutations of retrieved documents, even when the gold document is fixed. Its cluster-center aggregation over multiple permutations substantially improves consistency and accuracy. Contextual Normalization [arxiv:2510.13191v2] shows that seemingly superficial formatting decisions—delimiters, density, positional placement—systematically affect long-context reasoning, and that normalizing context layout improves robustness to order variation.

Multi-agent frameworks such as DRAG [arxiv:2505.18581v1] and VOTE-RAG [arxiv:2603.27253v2] implicitly define new evaluation axes: robustness to compounded hallucinations arising from erroneous retrieval and generation. Both use ensembles of agents and debate or voting schemes in retrieval and generation to reduce unsupported content, demonstrating improvements across multiple benchmarks. Surveys [arxiv:2510.24476v1; arxiv:2601.19927v1] argue that metrics capturing retrieval faithfulness, citation alignment, and robustness to noisy retrieval correlate better with hallucination risk than standard QA F1, but standardized, widely adopted grounding metrics (especially for long-form and multimodal outputs) are still lacking.

sections.section_title: 7. Safety, Privacy, and Security Risks in RAG
sections.content:
RAG systems introduce distinct safety, privacy, and security risks beyond those of standalone LLMs because they explicitly expose external knowledge bases. Formal analyses of RAG security [d41e12709c702b8568544ca3c6778d6116ac7e39] establish a threat model and taxonomy of adversaries, highlighting attack surfaces such as document-level membership inference and data poisoning. Membership inference surveys [4af7ef26d5efa62bf961087199bed3a3c8f1e653] and targeted RAG studies [21dc5aa608721f7e8a09e7dd9de8e48a71d9f0c9;17cef55807e90fdfe03e788d762d2432ca8654c4;arxiv:2502.00306v2] show that attackers can often infer whether specific documents reside in the retrieval database using carefully crafted prompts or differential calibration of responses, with high AUC and low query cost.

These attacks raise severe privacy concerns for systems built on proprietary or sensitive corpora (healthcare, finance). Participant-aware access control frameworks [4a6d3e5cb9f53005f97196a09469ca3de8e653] argue that probabilistic defenses like prompt sanitization or output filtering are insufficient; instead, deterministic, fine-grained authorization must be enforced across fine-tuning, retrieval, and generation. Federated RAG, as surveyed in [arxiv:2505.18906v2], introduces additional complexity: cross-client heterogeneity and federated learning protocols make it harder to track and control where data flows, while privacy-preserving retrieval and evaluation remain open problems.

Data poisoning in RAG—where adversaries inject malicious documents into the knowledge base—is formally modeled in [d41e12709c702b8568544ca3c6778d6116ac7e39] and is particularly dangerous because retrieval often bypasses traditional ML robustness defenses. Practical enterprise systems like Higress-RAG [arxiv:2602.23374v1] and Telco-RAG [arxiv:2404.15939v3] respond with layered architectures including retrieval filters, CRAG-style post-retrieval evaluation, and semantic caching, but robust, general-purpose defenses against poisoning and prompt injection via retrieved content remain immature.

Overall, current research demonstrates that RAG amplifies membership leakage and poisoning risks while also complicating access control. Stronger cryptographic, differential privacy, and provenance-tracking techniques have been proposed in adjacent literatures but are not yet standard in RAG deployments. Systematic benchmarks for security and privacy, analogous to performance benchmarks like KILT or Long^2RAG, are notably absent.

sections.section_title: 8. User-in-the-Loop and Interactive RAG
sections.content:
Interactive, user-in-the-loop RAG paradigms aim to refine retrieval and reasoning based on user feedback, clarification questions, or active learning signals. Agentic RAG surveys [arxiv:2601.05264v1; arxiv:2405.06211v3] and RAS frameworks [arxiv:2509.10697v1] highlight a shift from static retrieve-then-read pipelines toward multi-step, dialog-based orchestration.

Collab-RAG [arxiv:2504.04915v1] exemplifies white-box/black-box collaboration: a small, fine-tuned model decomposes complex questions into sub-questions for retrieval, while a larger black-box LLM answers and provides supervision feedback to improve decomposition. This demonstrates how user-like supervision (here, the black-box model) can iteratively refine retrieval strategy. FAIR-RAG [arxiv:2510.22344v1] goes further by introducing a Structured Evidence Assessment module that decomposes user queries into checklists of required findings, audits retrieved evidence for coverage, and triggers Adaptive Query Refinement to fill gaps before final generation—a form of automated, internal "user-in-the-loop" that approximates expert audit behavior.

Agentic platforms such as RAG-Gym [arxiv:2502.13957v2] provide environments to study how feedback signals—preference labels, step-level critiques—affect agent policies. REAPER [arxiv:2407.18553v2] and REAPER-like planners decouple retrieval planning from generation, allowing explicit multi-step retrieval plans that can be adapted to user corrections and new use cases without re-training classifiers. Domain-specific systems in fintech [arxiv:2510.25518v1] use specialized agents for query reformulation, acronym resolution, and sub-query decomposition; while not explicitly labeled as user-feedback systems, they are designed to accommodate iterative clarification and refinement over complex, jargon-heavy queries.

Trends suggest increasing use of checklists, intermediate reasoning traces, and planner modules that can be inspected and corrected by users or supervising agents. However, explicit work on active learning from user corrections, long-term adaptation to user preferences, and guarantees about convergence or safety of interactive policies is still sparse. Combining user feedback with robust retriever training and safety constraints remains an important frontier.

sections.section_title: 9. Cross-Lingual and Multilingual RAG
sections.content:
Although many RAG systems are developed for English, recent work has begun to tackle cross-lingual and multilingual scenarios. RA-LLM and RAS surveys [arxiv:2405.06211v3; arxiv:2509.10697v1] identify multilingual retrieval and structuring as key open challenges, especially for low-resource languages and noisy or domain-shifted corpora.

MiA-RAG [arxiv:2512.17220v1] evaluates its mindscape-aware RAG architecture on bilingual long-context benchmarks, showing that constructing a global semantic representation via hierarchical summarization can help align evidence across languages and improve global sense-making. MultiHop-RAG [arxiv:2401.15391v1], though focused on English news, highlights robustness issues in multi-hop retrieval that are likely exacerbated in multilingual settings, where cross-lingual alignment of embeddings and retrieval quality are more fragile.

Federated RAG, as surveyed in [arxiv:2505.18906v2], often involves heterogeneous clients with different languages and domains; this setting implicitly demands multilingual retrieval and generation, as well as privacy-preserving sharing of cross-lingual knowledge. Domain-specific systems such as Telco-RAG [arxiv:2404.15939v3] and MusT-RAG [arxiv:2507.23334v2] hint at multilingual usage scenarios (e.g., international standards, music metadata), though detailed multilingual evaluations are limited.

Overall, explicit cross-lingual RAG architectures—e.g., using multilingual encoders, cross-lingual indexing, and translation-in-the-loop retrieval—are still underrepresented in the literature compared to monolingual systems. Open problems include robust retrieval under code-switching, handling noisy OCR in multilingual documents, and reconciling domain-specific terminology across languages. Existing works suggest that global document representations (as in MiA-RAG) and structured indices (as in RAS frameworks) may be promising building blocks for multilingual RAG, but systematic benchmarks and evaluations are needed.

sections.section_title: 10. Deployment and MLOps for Production RAG Systems
sections.content:
Deploying RAG in production entails continuous index maintenance, monitoring, online evaluation, and cost/latency management. The Engineering the RAG Stack survey [arxiv:2601.05264v1] frames RAG as a multi-layer stack (retrieval, fusion, orchestration, governance) and emphasizes that system-level properties—robustness, latency, cost, and trust—must be evaluated alongside model-level metrics.

Enterprise-focused systems offer concrete design patterns. Higress-RAG [arxiv:2602.23374v1] implements dual hybrid retrieval (dense+sparse with Reciprocal Rank Fusion), semantic caching with dynamic thresholds, and Corrective RAG (CRAG) for post-retrieval evaluation within an MCP-based server, demonstrating that end-to-end optimization across query rewriting, retrieval, caching, and post-hoc hallucination control can improve precision and latency. T-RAG for enterprise documents [arxiv:2402.07483v2] and Telco-RAG [arxiv:2404.15939v3] report practical lessons: domain-specific document splitting, ontology-aware indexing, and fine-tuning on internal corpora are often necessary to achieve acceptable performance.

Agentic, multi-stage pipelines in fintech [arxiv:2510.25518v1] and collaborative multi-agent RAG over heterogeneous data sources [arxiv:2412.05838v1] illustrate MLOps challenges in orchestrating multiple agents and data connectors, including increased latency and complexity. REAPER [arxiv:2407.18553v2] proposes LLM-based retrieval planning as a way to reduce latency compared with general agent-style chains while retaining flexibility to add new data sources.

From an operations perspective, surveys on federated RAG [arxiv:2505.18906v2] and security/privacy [d41e12709c702b8568544ca3c6778d6116ac7e39;4a6d3e5cb9f53005f97196a09469ca3de8e653] stress the need for participant-aware access control, provenance tracking, and privacy-preserving retrieval. Yet, systematic practices for index refresh strategies, continuous retraining of retrievers and generators, online A/B testing and rollback, and cost/energy monitoring are still emerging. Many case studies rely on ad hoc solutions rather than standardized MLOps frameworks tailored to RAG.

sections.section_title: 11. Knowledge Stores, Indexing, and Chunking Strategies
sections.content:
The choice of knowledge store, ANN algorithms, metadata schemas, and chunking/compression strategies significantly affects retrieval quality and downstream generation. Surveys on RAG engineering [arxiv:2601.05264v1] and RAS [arxiv:2509.10697v1] categorize retrieval mechanisms into sparse, dense, and hybrid, and emphasize that hybrid strategies often yield better recall and robustness, especially in large or heterogeneous corpora.

Blended RAG [arxiv:2404.07220v2] showcases this by combining dense semantic search with sparse encoder indexes and hybrid query strategies, achieving improved top-k retrieval on NQ and TREC-COVID and better generative QA performance on SQuAD, sometimes surpassing fine-tuned models. Higress-RAG [arxiv:2602.23374v1] uses Reciprocal Rank Fusion over dense and sparse retrievers, coupled with a structure-aware document splitter, to boost retrieval precision for technical documentation. Telco-RAG [arxiv:2404.15939v3] and T-RAG for enterprise documents [arxiv:2402.07483v2] demonstrate that domain-specific splitting (respecting section boundaries, standards structure) is crucial; naive fixed-size chunking often harms both retrieval and generation.

For structured data, T-RAG over tables [arxiv:2504.01346v4] builds a hierarchical memory index capturing intra- and inter-table relationships and employs multi-stage retrieval to filter tables efficiently. MLDocRAG [arxiv:2602.10271v3] constructs a Multimodal Chunk-Query Graph linking heterogeneous document chunks to fine-grained queries. MiA-RAG [arxiv:2512.17220v1] and LongRAG [arxiv:2410.18050v2] illustrate how global document summaries (mindscapes, dual perspectives) can act as higher-level indices guiding local retrieval.

CARROT [arxiv:2411.00744v2] directly optimizes chunk selection under token budgets using MCTS and a learned utility estimator, modeling inter-chunk redundancy and ordering, and demonstrating up to 30% performance gains over top-k retrieval. Long^2RAG [arxiv:2410.23000v3] and Contextual Normalization [arxiv:2510.13191v2] show that effective chunking and normalization are intertwined with long-context utilization: key point coverage and stable formatting are critical for long-form grounding. A key research gap is principled methods for learning chunking and indexing jointly with retrievers and generators, particularly for multimodal and structured corpora.

sections.section_title: 12. Taxonomies of RAG Failure Modes and Robustness under Noisy Retrieval
sections.content:
Understanding RAG failure modes is essential for robust system design. Application-oriented surveys on hallucination [arxiv:2510.24476v1] and attribution [arxiv:2601.19927v1] propose taxonomies that distinguish retrieval miss (relevant documents not retrieved), reranking or selection errors, context-position sensitivity (lost-in-the-middle, permutation effects), reasoning failures (incorrect inference given correct evidence), and unsupported attribution (claims not grounded in retrieved content). These taxonomies help separate knowledge-based hallucinations (often due to retrieval) from logic-based ones (due to reasoning), guiding targeted interventions.

Empirical work such as MultiHop-RAG [arxiv:2401.15391v1] reveals that even when some relevant evidence is retrieved, systems often fail to chain multiple pieces, pointing to reasoning-level failures. Stable-RAG [arxiv:2601.02993v3] and Contextual Normalization [arxiv:2510.13191v2] show that context-position sensitivity and formatting can induce substantial variability in answers—even when retrieval is perfect—introducing a class of permutation-induced hallucinations. Long^2RAG [arxiv:2410.23000v3] highlights that LLMs frequently fail to incorporate key points from long retrieved documents, evidencing context underutilization.

Robustness to noisy or adversarial retrieval is addressed by several agentic and ensemble methods. DRAG [arxiv:2505.18581v1] embeds multi-agent debate in retrieval and generation so that proponents and opponents argue for or against candidate evidence, with judge agents selecting more reliable documents and answers, mitigating compounded hallucinations. VOTE-RAG [arxiv:2603.27253v2] uses parallel retrieval and response voting among multiple agents to aggregate noisy evidence and generations, showing competitive performance with fully parallelizable, training-free ensembles. FAIR-RAG [arxiv:2510.22344v1] explicitly identifies evidence gaps and avoids unsupported attribution by constraining final answers to verified evidence. Conformal factuality [arxiv:2603.16817v1] and probabilistic distance detectors [arxiv:2506.09886v2] focus on detecting low-confidence or hallucinated outputs under noisy retrieval.

Despite these advances, standardized robustness benchmarks that vary retrieval quality, introduce adversarial distractors, or simulate data poisoning are still limited. Integrating security-oriented threat models [d41e12709c702b8568544ca3c6778d6116ac7e39] with robustness taxonomies could yield more comprehensive evaluations of RAG under real-world noise and attacks.

sections.section_title: 13. Web-Integrated and Dynamically Updated RAG
sections.content:
RAG systems increasingly operate over live, web-scale knowledge sources rather than static corpora. While many of the cited works focus on static datasets, RA-LLM and RAS surveys [arxiv:2405.06211v3; arxiv:2509.10697v1] highlight emerging patterns for web-integrated RAG: dynamic crawling, indexing, and freshness-aware retrieval. EVOR [arxiv:2402.12317v2] provides a concrete instance in the code domain, where the knowledge base is continuously expanded with documentation and external resources to keep pace with evolving libraries and languages.

Enterprise systems such as Higress-RAG [arxiv:2602.23374v1] and Telco-RAG [arxiv:2404.15939v3] operate over rapidly changing technical documentation and standards, requiring regular ingestion, structure-aware splitting, and index refresh. Semantic caching and adaptive routing in Higress-RAG serve as mechanisms to balance freshness with latency and cost. Collaborative multi-agent RAG across diverse data [arxiv:2412.05838v1] emphasizes modular connectors to relational, NoSQL, document, and graph stores, enabling near-real-time access to heterogeneous, updated data.

Federated RAG [arxiv:2505.18906v2] adds another dynamic dimension by distributing knowledge across clients whose local data evolve independently, raising challenges for global consistency and evaluation. LongRAG [arxiv:2410.18050v2] and MiA-RAG [arxiv:2512.17220v1] show that global summarization and dual-perspective representations can help systems maintain coherent views over evolving long documents.

Compared to static-corpus RAG, web-integrated and dynamic systems must address staleness detection, index refresh policies, and robustness to uncurated web noise. While individual case studies suggest effective engineering patterns (semantic caching, routing, dynamic configuration agents as in CARROT [arxiv:2411.00744v2]), a systematic understanding of trade-offs between freshness, quality, and cost remains nascent, and standardized benchmarks for dynamic RAG are largely absent.

sections.section_title: 14. Personalization, Memory, and Session-Aware RAG
sections.content:
Personalization and session awareness in RAG seek to adapt retrieval and generation to user history, preferences, and permissions. While direct personalization-focused RAG works are underrepresented in the provided corpus, several strands of research are relevant. Federated RAG [arxiv:2505.18906v2] inherently involves per-client personalization: each client retains its own data, and federated learning aggregates knowledge while preserving privacy, enabling models that are adapted to client-specific distributions. Participant-aware access control [4a6d3e5cb9f53005f97196a09469ca3de8e653] emphasizes that personalized retrieval must respect fine-grained authorization, especially in multi-user enterprise settings.

Enterprise systems such as T-RAG [arxiv:2402.07483v2], Telco-RAG [arxiv:2404.15939v3], and fintech RAG [arxiv:2510.25518v1] implicitly rely on session context and user roles (e.g., engineer vs. product manager, trader vs. compliance officer) to shape retrieval and answer style. Higress-RAG [arxiv:2602.23374v1] uses semantic caching and adaptive routing that could be extended to session-aware caching keyed by user or task. Collaborative multi-agent RAG across diverse data [arxiv:2412.05838v1] enables agents specialized for different data sources, which could be conditioned on user profiles or long-term memory.

Surveys on RA-LLMs and RAS [arxiv:2405.06211v3; arxiv:2509.10697v1] highlight personalization and interactive adaptation as future directions, suggesting that structured representations and agentic orchestration can support user-specific memory and preference modeling. However, rigorous studies on how long-term memory stores, episodic retrieval, and preference learning interact with RAG are still limited. Key open questions include preventing overfitting to idiosyncratic user behavior, avoiding privacy leakage between users, and defining evaluation metrics for personalized RAG quality.

sections.section_title: 15. RAG vs. Alternative Knowledge Integration Strategies
sections.content:
RAG is one of several strategies for integrating external knowledge into LLMs, alongside fine-tuning, continual pre-training, knowledge editing, tool calling, and pure long-context modeling. Surveys on RA-LLMs [arxiv:2405.06211v3], RAG engineering [arxiv:2601.05264v1], and RAS [arxiv:2509.10697v1] compare these approaches conceptually: RAG decouples knowledge from parameters, enabling rapid updates and domain adaptation without full retraining, while fine-tuning and continual pre-training bake knowledge into the model’s weights, offering faster inference but slower updates and higher risk of catastrophic forgetting.

Empirically, Stronger Baselines for RAG with Long-Context LMs [arxiv:2506.03989v2] and RAG vs. LC comparisons [arxiv:2407.16833v2] show that well-engineered RAG can approach or match the performance of long-context models at lower cost, but may be outperformed when very large contexts and models are affordable. Domain-specific systems such as T-RAG [arxiv:2402.07483v2], Telco-RAG [arxiv:2404.15939v3], and MusT-RAG [arxiv:2507.23334v2] find that combining RAG with targeted fine-tuning of open-source LLMs often yields the best performance: fine-tuning adapts the generator to domain style and terminology, while RAG injects up-to-date content.

Knowledge editing and tool-calling approaches (e.g., executing SQL or code) intersect with RAG in multi-agent and RAS frameworks [arxiv:2509.10697v1; arxiv:2412.05838v1], where retrieval may involve querying structured databases or APIs rather than static text. EVOR [arxiv:2402.12317v2] demonstrates that RAG over dynamically evolving code documentation complements parametric knowledge and traditional code fine-tuning, especially in rapidly changing libraries. Federated RAG [arxiv:2505.18906v2] shows that decoupling knowledge from model parameters is essential in privacy-sensitive settings where centralizing data for fine-tuning is impossible.

In practice, RAG is preferable when (1) the knowledge base is large, dynamic, or sensitive; (2) real-time grounding and provenance are required; or (3) multi-modal or structured data must be integrated. Fine-tuning and continual pre-training remain valuable for compressing frequently used knowledge and adapting the model’s style or reasoning patterns. Long-context models excel when the task involves a small number of very long documents or when retrieval is unreliable. The most promising trend is toward hybrid systems—agentic RAG with selective fine-tuning, LC routing, and tool use—that leverage the strengths of each strategy while mitigating their weaknesses.
